title: Bouillabaisse
subtitle: Provençal fish and seafood stew
excerpt: A Provençal stew of multiple fish and shellfish cooked in a saffron-and-fennel broth, served with rouille on toasted bread. The defining dish of Marseille — lavish, aromatic, and better at home than in most restaurants.
sourceNotes: Bouillabaisse originates in Marseille, where fishermen cooked their unsellable catch in a saffron broth. The canonical recipe is documented in Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, and Simone Beck, Mastering the Art of French Cooking (1961) and Elizabeth David, French Provincial Cooking (1960). A 1980 Marseille charter specified which fish must be included in a 'true' bouillabaisse.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The broth base can be made up to 1 day ahead. Add fish only when ready to serve — do not reheat with fish already in it.

content.text: Bouillabaisse requires variety — at least three kinds of fish, and ideally some shellfish alongside. The interplay of different textures and flavours is what makes it a bouillabaisse rather than a fish soup. The saffron-and-fennel broth is the constant; the fish changes according to what the market has.

The traditional accompaniment is rouille — a thick, garlicky, saffron-scented mayonnaise spread on rounds of toasted bread that float in the bowl. A simplified rouille can be made by blending good mayonnaise with a crushed garlic clove, a pinch of saffron, and a squeeze of lemon. Serve the broth first, then the fish and bread separately, or bring everything to the table at once.

items.prepNote: filleted and cut into large pieces

content.text: Heat the olive oil in a large pot over medium heat. Add the onion, fennel, and garlic and cook for 10 minutes until soft. Add the tomatoes, fennel seeds, bay leaves, and thyme and cook for 5 minutes. Add 1.5 litres of cold water, the saffron water, and the potatoes. Bring to a boil and cook at a rapid simmer for 15 minutes until the potatoes are just tender. Season well.

Add the firmest fish first (cod) and cook for 2 minutes. Add the sea bass and prawns and cook for 2 more minutes. Add the mussels, cover the pot, and cook for 3 to 4 minutes until the mussels have opened and all the fish is just cooked through. Do not overcook — remove from the heat as soon as everything is done.

Ladle into large warm bowls, making sure each person gets a mix of fish and shellfish. Scatter with parsley and fennel fronds. Serve with rouille on toasted bread placed in or alongside the bowl, and lemon wedges. A plain rouille: mash 2 garlic cloves to a paste with salt, mix with 4 tablespoons of good mayonnaise, a large pinch of saffron, and a squeeze of lemon.

items.symptom: Fish is overcooked and falling apart
items.cause: Added too early or broth was boiling when fish went in
items.fix: Add fish to a simmering (not boiling) broth; total cooking time for fish is 6 to 8 minutes from the point of adding the first fish

items.cause: Not enough saffron or fennel; or potatoes absorbed too much flavour
items.fix: Add a second large pinch of saffron steeped in warm water; the fennel is also essential — don't omit it

items.cause: Not enough heat, or insufficient time
items.fix: Cover the pot and cook at a full simmer for 4 to 5 minutes; discard any mussels that remain closed after 6 minutes

content.text: Bouillabaisse belongs to Marseille the way cassoulet belongs to Toulouse. The word describes both a dish and a method (bouillir, to boil, and abaisser, to reduce), and the traditional version was made from the unsellable fish left after market: rockfish, rascasse, grondin. The home version uses whatever good fish is available. The Marseille charter of 1980, which defined which fish were obligatory, was aimed at tourist restaurants claiming authenticity — at home, use the best fish you can find.
